summary: Matt Rocklin explores why startup CEOs project inevitable success despite most startups failing. He attributes this to a combination of overconfidence from past success and pressure to maintain team morale. The post frames this as a tradeoff: someone in the organization must bear the psychological cost of uncertainty, either the CEO through strategic dishonesty or the team through accepting potential failure. Rocklin personally chooses honesty, not from ethics but because dishonesty is emotionally stressful for him, acknowledging this filters for like-minded colleagues.
key_points: Most CEOs projecting success despite high failure rates stems from both overconfidence and pressure to maintain morale
Uncertainty carries a psychological cost that must be borne by someone in the organization
CEOs often absorb uncertainty by spinning or slightly misrepresenting reality to protect their teams
Personal honesty about failure potential filters for colleagues with realistic worldviews
money_quotes: Most startups fail, and yet most CEOs project inevitable success. What gives?
There is a psychological cost to uncertainty or possible failure that someone in the organization has to pay.
I try to be an honest person. This isn't due to any kind of ethics; I just find dishonesty to be emotionally very stressful.
I live a better life when I don't hold anything back.
This often has negative consequences, but overall it filters for colleagues who have a frank and realistic view of the world.
key_insight: The psychological burden of startup uncertainty must be carried by someone—either the CEO absorbs it through strategic optimism, or the team confronts it directly.
